content.text: Literature review agents automate the systematic process of surveying academic databases, screening papers for relevance, extracting key findings, assessing study quality, and synthesising evidence into structured reports. They compress weeks of manual systematic review work into hours by applying consistent inclusion criteria, structured extraction templates, and quality assessment rubrics at scale across hundreds or thousands of papers.

attrs.alt: Literature Review Agent Pipeline: topic → query builder → database search → screening → full-text extraction → quality assessment → evidence map → synthesis report

content.text: What Is a Literature Review Agent?

A literature review agent is an AI system that follows a structured, reproducible methodology for surveying academic literature on a research question. It implements the key stages of a systematic review — database search, title-abstract screening, full-text extraction, quality assessment, and evidence synthesis — as an automated pipeline. The agent reads and processes academic papers, applies defined inclusion and exclusion criteria, extracts structured data fields (study design, population, intervention, outcome, effect size), and produces a report that conforms to reporting standards such as PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses).

Unlike a deep research agent that searches broadly across all information types, a literature review agent is specifically optimised for academic literature. It understands the structure of academic papers (abstract, methods, results, discussion, limitations), uses domain-specific search strategies (Boolean queries, MeSH terms, controlled vocabularies), and applies research methodology quality criteria (risk-of-bias assessment, sample size, confounders, replication).

The most common application domains are biomedical research (systematic reviews for clinical guidelines), social science (evidence-based policy reports), and technology assessment (evaluating the evidence base for a new method or tool). In each domain, the agent reduces the manual burden of screening and data extraction while maintaining reproducibility through logged search strategies and applied criteria.

A manual systematic review for a clinical guideline can take a team of 3–5 researchers six to twelve months. The bottleneck is not searching — it is screening thousands of titles and abstracts, full-text reviewing hundreds of papers, and extracting structured data from each. A literature review agent can screen 5,000 abstracts in under an hour and extract structured data from full-text PDFs at a rate of 30–60 papers per minute, reducing the overall review timeline from months to days.

Reproducibility is a core value of systematic reviews: the search strategy, inclusion criteria, and extraction protocol must be documented and repeatable. An automated agent applies criteria identically to every paper, eliminating the inter-reviewer variability that occurs when multiple human reviewers apply slightly different interpretations of the same inclusion rule. This improves the reliability of the review findings.
Speed: screens thousands of abstracts per hour vs. hundreds per day for human reviewers.
Consistency: applies the same criteria identically to every paper, eliminating inter-reviewer variability.
Reproducibility: logs every search query, inclusion decision, and extraction output for audit and replication.
Breadth: searches multiple databases simultaneously (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, arXiv, Semantic Scholar) in a single run.
Structured extraction: outputs machine-readable data tables for downstream meta-analysis or visualisation.
Scalability: the same agent handles reviews with 100 papers and reviews with 10,000 papers without workflow changes.

Literature review agents achieve high recall (they rarely miss relevant papers) but may have lower precision than expert human reviewers, particularly for papers with ambiguous relevance or methodological edge cases. Setting strict inclusion criteria improves precision but risks excluding relevant studies; liberal criteria capture more papers but increase manual review burden downstream.

Full-text access is a persistent barrier. Most papers are paywalled; the agent can reliably access abstracts from PubMed and Semantic Scholar but may be blocked from full-text extraction for 60–70% of relevant papers without institutional access. OpenAccess APIs (Unpaywall, PubMed Central) partially address this for biomedical literature but coverage is incomplete in other domains.
Paywall limitations: full-text extraction fails for papers without open access.
PDF parsing challenges: multi-column layouts, tables, and figures in PDFs are error-prone to extract with standard text parsers.
Domain specificity: quality assessment rubrics (RCT risk-of-bias, Newcastle-Ottawa scale) must be configured per review type; a generic agent lacks these by default.
No access to unpublished data: grey literature (conference abstracts, government reports, theses) requires separate search strategies.
Language bias: most agents work only in English; multilingual reviews require additional translation infrastructure.

The pipeline begins with query construction. The agent translates the PICO framework (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome) or a free-text research question into Boolean search strings adapted to each database's controlled vocabulary. PubMed queries use MeSH terms; Embase uses Emtree; arXiv uses category and keyword filters. The agent generates database-specific queries for each target source and executes them in parallel.

The screening step applies inclusion and exclusion criteria to titles and abstracts. The criteria are encoded as a structured prompt: study design requirements (must be RCT, cohort, or case-control), population requirements (human subjects, adult participants), intervention requirements, and exclusion terms (animal studies, non-English, conference abstracts only). The LLM classifies each paper as include, exclude, or uncertain, with a justification.

For included papers, full-text extraction uses PDF parsing (PyMuPDF, pdfplumber) to retrieve the full text, then structured prompts to extract defined data fields into a table. A quality assessment step applies the appropriate rubric (Cochrane Risk-of-Bias for RCTs, GRADE for evidence certainty) to each included study. The final synthesis step clusters studies by theme, identifies consensus findings and contradictions, and writes the structured report.

The pipeline above shows the six-stage literature review architecture. The Query Builder generates database-specific Boolean search strings. Database Search executes against multiple academic APIs in parallel. Screening applies inclusion/exclusion criteria to titles and abstracts, with included papers passed to Full-text Extraction. Quality Assessment applies study design rubrics. The Evidence Map clusters findings by theme. The Synthesis Report integrates all stages into a PRISMA-compliant output.
Systematic reviews for clinical guidelines or policy recommendations.
Rapid evidence reviews: quickly scanning literature on an emerging topic to inform a decision.
Technology assessments: evaluating the evidence base for a proposed new method or tool.
Research gap identification: finding areas with insufficient evidence to prioritise new research.
Grant application background sections: constructing a comprehensive literature summary.
Market research informed by academic findings.

Not suitable for: reviews requiring access to unpublished data, reviews needing expert clinical judgment on complex methodological questions, or reviews of non-text sources (e.g., clinical images or instruments).
Example 2 — Semantic Scholar Search and Extraction

import httpx
import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def semantic_scholar_search(query: str, limit: int = 50) -> list[dict]:
    """Search Semantic Scholar (free, no key required for basic use)."""
    resp = httpx.get(
        "https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search",
        params={
            "query": query,
            "fields": "title,abstract,year,authors,externalIds,openAccessPdf",
            "limit": limit,
        },
        timeout=30,
    )
    return resp.json().get("data", [])

def batch_screen(papers: list[dict], criteria: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Screen a batch of papers using a single LLM call (cheaper)."""
    papers_text = "
".join([
        f"{i+1}. {p['title']} ({p.get('year','?')})
   {(p.get('abstract') or '')[:200]}"
        for i, p in enumerate(papers[:20])  # batch of 20
    ])

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Screen these papers for inclusion. Criteria: {criteria}

Papers:
{papers_text}

Return JSON list: [{{"index": 1, "include": true/false, "reason": "brief"}}]
"""}]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    start = text.find("[")
    end = text.rfind("]") + 1
    decisions = json.loads(text[start:end]) if start >= 0 else []
    return [papers[d["index"]-1] for d in decisions if d.get("include")]

# Run a mini literature review
papers = semantic_scholar_search(
    "transformer language models instruction following",
    limit=100
)
print(f"Retrieved {len(papers)} papers")

included = batch_screen(papers[:80], "empirical studies on LLM instruction tuning, published 2022-2024")
print(f"Included: {len(included)}")

# Extract key themes
themes_resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=2048,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Identify 3-5 key themes from these included papers and summarise findings per theme.

Papers: {json.dumps([{"title": p["title"], "abstract": (p.get("abstract") or "")[:300]} for p in included[:20]])}
"""}]
)
print(themes_resp.content[0].text)

The Cochrane Collaboration, which produces the gold standard of systematic reviews for medical evidence, has piloted AI-assisted screening tools that apply machine learning classifiers trained on previous Cochrane reviews to screen PubMed search results. In a validation study, an AI screener achieved 95.2% recall (missing only 4.8% of relevant papers) while eliminating 60% of papers as clearly irrelevant before human review, reducing the human screening burden by more than half.

A pharmaceutical company uses a literature review agent to maintain living systematic reviews of their pipeline drugs — automatically updating the review as new papers are published. The agent monitors PubMed for papers matching the drug name and MeSH terms, screens new additions weekly, extracts adverse event data into a structured database, and flags any papers reporting new safety signals for immediate review. This replaces a team of 4 medical information specialists who previously performed monthly manual sweeps.
Single database search: searching only PubMed misses papers in Embase, CENTRAL, PsycINFO, and grey literature.
Inconsistent inclusion criteria: vague criteria like 'high-quality studies' are applied differently across papers; use specific operationalised criteria.
Ignoring duplicate detection: the same paper often appears in multiple databases; deduplicate by DOI or title similarity before screening.
No backward/forward citation tracking: papers not retrieved by the initial search but cited by or citing included papers may be highly relevant.
Over-relying on abstracts: abstract content often misrepresents the study; budget for full-text review of borderline includes.
Failing to log decisions: without an audit log of every inclusion/exclusion decision and its reason, the review cannot be reproduced or challenged.
Register the review protocol (PROSPERO for medical reviews) before data collection to prevent outcome reporting bias.
Search at least three databases; for biomedical topics include PubMed, Embase, and CENTRAL as a minimum.
Apply deduplication before screening — cross-database duplicates inflate workload and bias inclusion rates.
Use a PICO framework to operationalise inclusion criteria before running any searches.
Run backward citation tracking on all included papers to capture seminal works missed by database searches.
Log every screening decision with a justification string for reproducibility and audit.
Output structured extraction tables in machine-readable format (JSON or CSV) to enable downstream meta-analysis.

Screening is the dominant performance bottleneck for large reviews. A single LLM call to screen one abstract takes 300–600 ms; screening 5,000 abstracts sequentially takes 25–50 minutes. Batching 10–20 abstracts per LLM call (as shown in the batch_screen example above) reduces total screening time to 3–8 minutes for 5,000 papers. The batching approach is feasible because inclusion decisions are largely independent, and the model handles up to 20 papers per call reliably.
Free; structured XML output; MEDLINE coverage
Free; resolves DOI to OA full text where available
Handles columns, tables in academic PDFs
Import agent-screened decisions for expert override

Literature review agents query external academic APIs that may be rate-limited, temporarily unavailable, or may return inconsistent results across time. Build retry logic with exponential backoff for all API calls. Cache search results locally so that a pipeline failure mid-way through can be resumed without re-running expensive searches. Store raw search result JSON alongside the final report so the search strategy can be reproduced.
Validate that the agent's inclusion/exclusion decisions match human reviewer decisions on a held-out validation set before deploying for high-stakes reviews.
Run deduplication before screening to prevent the same paper appearing multiple times and being included twice.
Implement a confidence threshold: papers classified as 'uncertain' by the screening model should be flagged for human review rather than automatically excluded.
Sanitise extracted text from PDFs before passing to the LLM — PDFs can contain malformed characters or injected text.
For medical or regulatory reviews, require human expert review of all included papers before any policy decisions are made based on the synthesis.
Literature review agents automate the systematic review pipeline: database search, abstract screening, full-text extraction, quality assessment, and synthesis.
Batching abstract screening (10-20 papers per LLM call) reduces screening time by 10× compared to sequential per-paper calls.
Multi-database coverage (PubMed, Embase, Semantic Scholar, arXiv) is essential; single-database reviews miss significant proportions of relevant literature.
Every inclusion/exclusion decision must be logged with a justification for reproducibility and audit.
Paywall restrictions limit full-text access to 30-40% of papers without institutional subscriptions; Unpaywall and PubMed Central partially compensate.
For high-stakes medical or regulatory reviews, AI-screened results must be validated against human reviewer decisions before deployment.
PICO framework operationalisation of inclusion criteria is the key to consistent, automatable screening.
